slides.title: The Kitchen Analogy 🧑‍🍳
slides.content:
## Sequential vs. Concurrent

Imagine making breakfast. 

**Sequential**: You toast the bread. *Then* you wait. *Then* you fry the eggs. *Then* you wait. 

**Concurrent**: You put the bread in the toaster. While it's toasting, you start frying the eggs! You are managing two tasks overlapping in time. This is concurrency.

quizData.question: What is the core idea of 'Concurrency' in programming?

options.text: Running code exactly line-by-line, waiting for each step to finish.
Managing multiple tasks at once so they can overlap in time.
Making code run faster by skipping unnecessary lines.

slides.title: Why Go is Famous 🌟
slides.content:
## Built for the Modern Web

Many older languages struggle with concurrency. They use heavy "Threads" that use a lot of computer memory.

Go was designed at Google *specifically* to handle thousands of concurrent tasks (like thousands of users hitting a web server at the exact same moment) incredibly efficiently.
